--- Page 1 ---
arXiv:2603.00130v1  [cs.MA]  23 Feb 2026
Agentic Hives: Equilibrium, Indeterminacy,
and Endogenous Cycles
in Self-Organizing Multi-Agent Systems
Jean-Philippe GarnierŌłŚ
BrainiaK
Gap, France
February 2026
Abstract
Current multi-agent AI systems operate with a ’¼üxed number of agents whose
roles are speci’¼üed at design time. No formal theory governs when agents should
be created, destroyed, or re-specialized at runtimeŌĆölet alone how the population
structure responds to changes in resources or objectives.
We introduce the Agentic Hive, a framework in which a variable population of
autonomous micro-agentsŌĆöeach equipped with a sandboxed execution environment
and access to a language modelŌĆöundergoes demographic dynamics: birth, duplica-
tion, specialization, and death. Agent families play the role of production sectors,
compute and memory play the role of factors of production, and an orchestrator
plays the dual role of Walrasian auctioneer and Global Workspace.
Drawing on the multi-sector growth theory developed for dynamic general equi-
librium (Benhabib & Nishimura, 1985; Venditti, 2005; Garnier, Nishimura & Ven-
ditti, 2013), we prove seven analytical results: (i) existence of a Hive Equilibrium
via BrouwerŌĆÖs ’¼üxed-point theorem; (ii) Pareto optimality of the equilibrium alloca-
tion; (iii) multiplicity of equilibria under strategic complementarities between agent
families; (iv)ŌĆō(v) StolperŌĆōSamuelson and Rybczynski analogs that predict how the
Hive restructures in response to preference and resource shocks; (vi) Hopf bifurca-
tion generating endogenous demographic cycles; and (vii) a su’¼ācient condition for
local asymptotic stability.
The resulting regime diagram partitions the parameter space into regions of
unique equilibrium, indeterminacy, endogenous cycles, and instability.
Together
with the comparative-statics matrices, it provides a formal governance toolkit that
enables operators to predict and steer the demographic evolution of self-organizing
multi-agent systems.
Keywords:
multi-agent systems, general equilibrium, dynamic general equilib-
rium, agent demographics, indeterminacy, endogenous cycles, Hopf bifurcation, LLM
orchestration.
ŌłŚCorresponding author. E-mail: jeanphi@brainiak.ai.
1

--- Page 3 ---
1
Introduction
The past two years have witnessed an explosion of multi-agent systems built around large
language models (LLMs). Systems such as AutoGen [29], CrewAI [5], MetaGPT [14], and
Swarm [24] enable several LLM-powered agents to collaborate on complex tasks.
All of these systems share a common architectural assumption: the number of agents is
’¼üxed at design time, and their roles are pre-assigned. The system designer decides that
there will be a ŌĆ£plannerŌĆØ agent, a ŌĆ£coderŌĆØ agent, and a ŌĆ£reviewerŌĆØ agent, then hard-codes
the interaction protocol. If the task mix changes, or if resources become scarce, or if one
agent family becomes redundant, the system has no principled mechanism for adaptation.
This is the equivalent of running an economy without a theory of growth: you can allo-
cate resources across existing ’¼ürms, but you have no formal basis for deciding when new
’¼ürms should enter, when existing ones should exit, or how the industrial structure should
respond to a technology shock.
1.1
The missing theory
We argue that the missing ingredient is a macroeconomic theory of agent demographics.
Speci’¼ücally, we need formal answers to four questions:
(Q1) Entry: When should new agents be created, and of what type?
(Q2) Exit: When should existing agents be removed?
(Q3) Specialization: How should undi’¼Ćerentiated agents acquire functional roles?
(Q4) Restructuring: How does the entire population structure respond to changes in
resources or objectives?
No existing framework provides analytical answers to (Q1)ŌĆō(Q4). Swarm intelligence [7,
18] studies self-organization but provides no equilibrium theory. Multi-agent reinforcement
learning (MARL) [19] learns policies but does not model population dynamics. Agent-
based computational economics (ACE) [26] simulates agent populations but o’¼Ćers no
closed-form results.
1.2
Our approach
We observe that (Q1)ŌĆō(Q4) are precisely the questions addressed by the theory of multi-
sector economic growthŌĆöa branch of dynamic general equilibrium theory that has been
developed over seven decades, from Arrow & Debreu [1], Debreu [4], and McKenzie [20]
to Garnier, Nishimura & Venditti [10].
The mapping is direct:
3


--- Page 4 ---
Multi-sector economy
Agentic Hive
Production sectors
Agent families (perception, planning, . . . )
Factors (capital, labor)
Resources (GPU, memory, attention, . . . )
Output of sector j
Task completion by family j
Factor prices
Shadow prices of resources
Consumer preferences
System objectives (quality, cost, latency, . . . )
Walrasian auctioneer
Orchestrator / Global Workspace
Inter-sectoral externalities
Cross-family spillovers
Steady-state population
Hive Equilibrium
This is not a metaphor. As we show formally in Section 3, the mathematical objects
are identical:
the population dynamics of agent families are governed by a dynami-
cal system whose steady states are general equilibria, whose comparative statics obey
StolperŌĆōSamuelson and Rybczynski theorems, and whose stability properties determine
the existence of endogenous demographic cycles.
1.3
Contributions
We introduce the Agentic Hive, a formal framework for self-organizing multi-agent systems
with variable population, and establish the following results:
1. Existence (Theorem 4.1): Under standard regularity assumptions, at least one
Hive Equilibrium exists.
2. Pareto optimality (Theorem 4.2): Every Hive Equilibrium is Pareto-optimal when
the orchestrator has full information.
3. Multiplicity (Theorem 4.4): Under strategic complementarities between agent fam-
ilies, multiple equilibria coexistŌĆöcorresponding to distinct ŌĆ£morphologiesŌĆØ of the
Hive.
4. StolperŌĆōSamuelson analog (Theorem 4.6): A magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect links prefer-
ence changes to resource price changes.
5. Rybczynski analog (Theorem 4.8): A magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect links resource endow-
ment changes to population restructuring.
6. Endogenous cycles (Theorem 4.10): A Hopf bifurcation generates periodic demo-
graphic oscillationsŌĆöthe ŌĆ£seasonsŌĆØ of the Hive.
7. Stability (Theorem 4.12): A su’¼ācient condition on mortality rates guarantees local
asymptotic stability.
Together, these results yield a regime diagram (Section 6) that partitions the parameter
space into regions of qualitatively distinct behavior, providing a formal governance toolkit
for designers and operators of multi-agent systems.
1.4
Relation to prior work by the author
The authorŌĆÖs doctoral work [9, 10] characterized local indeterminacy in continuous-time
multi-sector growth models, establishing conditions under which competitive equilibrium
cycles arise from the interaction of returns to scale and consumer preferences. The present
paper transports this toolkit to the entirely di’¼Ćerent domain of multi-agent AI systems,
4


--- Page 5 ---
extending it from ’¼üxed-population general equilibrium to variable populations with en-
dogenous demographics and multi-regime dynamics.
2
Related work
Multi-agent LLM systems.
AutoGen [29], MetaGPT [14], CrewAI [5], and Swarm [24]
enable multi-agent collaboration with LLMs. All assume a ’¼üxed agent set with pre-de’¼üned
roles. Recent surveys [12] catalog the rapid growth of this ’¼üeld but do not identify popu-
lation dynamics as a research gap.
Swarm intelligence.
Ant Colony Optimization [7], Particle Swarm Optimization [18],
and related algorithms use local interaction rules to achieve collective behavior. These ap-
proaches do not provide equilibrium concepts, comparative statics, or stability theorems.
Evolutionary game theory.
The replicator equation [13] governs population shares
under frequency-dependent ’¼ütness. Our population dynamics (Section 3.4) are a general-
ization of replicator dynamics to absolute populations with endogenous resource allocation
and multi-sector externalities.
Agent-based computational economics.
ACE [26] simulates heterogeneous agent
populations but produces no closed-form results. Our framework provides analytical the-
orems that complement simulation.
Multi-sector growth theory.
Benhabib & Nishimura [3] established the existence of
competitive equilibrium cycles in multi-sector optimal growth models. Venditti [28] and
Garnier, Nishimura & Venditti [10] extended these results to characterize indeterminacy
under various assumptions on returns to scale and preferences. We transport this entire
toolkit to agent demographics.
Global Workspace Theory.
Baars [2] proposed that consciousness arises from a global
workspace that integrates and broadcasts information from specialist processors. Our or-
chestrator plays an analogous role: it selects, integrates, and broadcasts resource alloca-
tion signals across agent families.
3
The Agentic Hive model
3.1
Agents, families, and resources
De’¼ünition 3.1 (Agentic Hive). An Agentic Hive is a tuple H = (At, F, R, w, ╬ō, ╬ś)
where:
ŌĆó At is the set of agents alive at time t, with |At| = Ntotal(t) variable;
ŌĆó F = {1, . . . , S} is a ’¼ünite set of agent families (functional specializations);
ŌĆó R = (R1, . . . , RM) ŌłłRM
+ is the vector of resource endowments;
ŌĆó w = (w1, . . . , wS) ŌłłŌłåS is the vector of global preferences over family outputs;
ŌĆó ╬ō = (╬│jk)j,k ŌłłRS├ŚS is the externality matrix;
5


--- Page 6 ---
ŌĆó ╬ś is the orchestrator.
We denote by Nj
t ŌłłR+ the population of family j at time t, and by Nt = (N1
t , . . . , NS
t ) Ōłł
RS
+ the population vector.
De’¼ünition 3.2 (Agent). An agent a ŌłłAt is a tuple a = (ga, Žāa, fa, ┬Ąa, Žäa, Žēa, ╬Ča) where:
ŌĆó ga ŌłłG: genome (base prompt, policy, con’¼üguration);
ŌĆó Žāa Ōłł╬Ż: internal state (local memory, context);
ŌĆó fa ŌłłF Ōł¬{Ōłģ}: family assignment (Ōłģfor stem agents);
ŌĆó ┬Ąa ŌłłRK
+: performance pro’¼üle (K metrics);
ŌĆó Žäa ŌłłR+: age;
ŌĆó Žēa: sandboxed execution environment;
ŌĆó ╬Ča Ōłł[0, 1]: degree of specialization (0 = totipotent, 1 = fully specialized).
De’¼ünition 3.3 (Stem agent). An agent a is a stem agent if ╬Ča = 0 and fa = Ōłģ. A stem
agent can duplicate (create a copy with reset state) or specialize (transition to a family
j ŌłłF with ╬Ča > 0).
Resources are organized into M types.
Each agent in family j consumes a resource
vector; the aggregate consumption by family j of resource m is denoted Km
j . The matrix
K = (Km
j )j,m ŌłłRS├ŚM
+
records the full resource allocation.
Assumption 1 (Budget). Total population is bounded by a budget constraint: PS
j=1 cj Nj Ōēż
B, where cj > 0 is the per-agent maintenance cost of family j and B > 0 is the total
budget.
De’¼üne the feasible population set:
Ōä”=

+ : PS

(1)
Ōä”is compact and convex.
3.2
Production and externalities
Each family produces output that depends on its resource allocation, its population, and
spillovers from other families.
De’¼ünition 3.4 (Sectoral production). The output of family j is:
Yj(Kj, Nj, NŌłÆj) = Aj ┬Ę Fj(Kj) ┬Ę (Nj)╬Ęj ┬Ę ╬ōj(NŌłÆj),
(2)
where:
ŌĆó Aj > 0 is the total factor productivity of family j;
ŌĆó Fj : RM
+ ŌåÆR+ is a CES production function in resources:
Fj(Kj) =

(3)
with ╬▒jm > 0, P
m ╬▒jm = 1, and Žüj > 0 the elasticity of substitution;
6


--- Page 7 ---
ŌĆó (Nj)╬Ęj captures returns to scale within the family, with ╬Ęj > 0;
ŌĆó ╬ōj(NŌłÆj) = Q
k╠Ė=j(Nk)╬│jk is the externality term, with ╬│jk ŌłłR.
Assumption 2 (Production regularity). For each j: (i) Fj is C2, increasing, and strictly
concave; (ii) Inada conditions hold: ŌłéFj/ŌłéKm
j
ŌåÆŌł×as Km
j
ŌåÆ0+ and ŌłéFj/ŌłéKm
j
ŌåÆ0
as Km
j
ŌåÆŌł×; (iii) 0 < ╬Ęj < 1 (decreasing returns within family; relaxed to ╬Ęj Ōēź1 in
Section 4.3).
Remark 3.5. The assumption ╬Ęj < 1 guarantees that the marginal value of an additional
agent diminishes within each family, ensuring interior equilibria. In Theorem 4.4, we relax
this to ╬Ęj Ōēź1 for some families, which is the source of indeterminacy.
The externality matrix E = (╬│jk) is the central structural object of the theory:
ŌĆó ╬│jk > 0: family k generates a positive spillover on family j (complementarity);
ŌĆó ╬│jk < 0: family k imposes a negative externality on family j (congestion);
ŌĆó ╬│jk = 0: independence.
3.3
Social welfare and the orchestratorŌĆÖs problem
De’¼ünition 3.6 (Social welfare). The social welfare function is:
W(K, N) =
S
X
j=1
wj ┬Ę u

Yj(Kj, Nj, NŌłÆj)

ŌłÆ
S
X
j=1
cj Nj,
(4)
where u : R+ ŌåÆR is a CRRA utility: u(Y ) = Y 1ŌłÆŽā/(1 ŌłÆŽā) for Žā > 0, Žā ╠Ė= 1, and
u(Y ) = ln Y for Žā = 1.
De’¼ünition 3.7 (OrchestratorŌĆÖs problem). Given population N, the orchestrator solves
the inner problem:
W ŌłŚ(N) = max
KŌēź0 W(K, N)
s.t.
S
X
j=1
Km
j
ŌēżRm
ŌłĆm Ōłł{1, . . ., M}.
(5)
Assumption 3 (Utility regularity). u is C2, strictly increasing, and strictly concave
on R++.
Assumption 4 (Weak externalities). The externality matrix satis’¼ües
ŌłźEŌłźŌł×= max

,
(6)
where ╬Ęmax = maxj ╬Ęj < 1 (from Assumption 2(iii)) and Žā is the CRRA parameter.
Remark 3.8. Assumption 4 ensures that the value function W ŌłŚ(N) is globally concave
on Ōä”. The bound arises because the Hessian of W ŌłŚwith respect to N has diagonal terms
of order ╬Ęj ŌłÆ1 < 0 (diminishing returns) and o’¼Ć-diagonal terms of order ╬│jk (externalities).
Concavity holds when the negative diagonal dominates the o’¼Ć-diagonal perturbation. This
assumption is relaxed in Section 4.3 to obtain indeterminacy.
Under Assumptions 2 and 3, the inner problem (5) has a unique solution KŌłŚ(N) =
(KmŌłŚ
j (N))j,m, with associated shadow prices ╬╗ŌłŚ(N) = (╬╗1ŌłŚ, . . . , ╬╗MŌłŚ) satisfying the ’¼ürst-

(7)
De’¼ünition 3.9 (Marginal social value). The marginal social value of an agent in family j
is:
Vj(N) = d W ŌłŚ

3.4
Population dynamics
The demographic dynamics of the Hive are governed by a system of ODEs:
De’¼ünition 3.10 (Population dynamics). The population of family j evolves according
to:
dNj
dt
= ╬”j(N) = Vj(N) ┬Ę Nj,
j = 1, . . . , S.
(10)
This is a selection dynamic [13]: families with positive marginal value (Vj > 0) grow;
families with negative marginal value (Vj < 0) decline.
In vector form: ╦ÖN = ╬”(N), where ╬” : RS
+ ŌåÆRS.
Remark 3.11 (Interpretation of Vj). The marginal social value Vj aggregates three forces:
(i) Direct returns: an additional agent in family j increases Yj through the term (Nj)╬Ęj;
(ii) Externalities: through ╬ōj(NŌłÆj), the output of family j depends on the populations
of other families;
(iii) Cost: the maintenance cost cj reduces Vj.
When Vj = 0, family j is at demographic equilibrium: the marginal bene’¼üt of an additional
agent exactly equals its cost.
Remark 3.12 (Relation to replicator dynamics). Equation (10) is a generalization of the
replicator equation to absolute populations with endogenous ’¼ütness. In the classical repli-
cator equation, ╦Öxi = xi(fi ŌłÆ┬»f) with exogenous ’¼ütness fi. Here, the ŌĆ£’¼ütnessŌĆØ Vj(N) is
endogenous: it depends on all populations through the production functions, externalities,
and the orchestratorŌĆÖs optimal allocation.
3.5
Hive Equilibrium
De’¼ünition 3.13 (Hive Equilibrium). A Hive Equilibrium is a triple (NŌłŚ, KŌłŚ, ╬╗ŌłŚ) such
that:
(HE-1) Allocation optimality: KŌłŚsolves the inner problem (5) for NŌłŚ, with shadow
prices ╬╗ŌłŚ;
8


--- Page 9 ---
(HE-2) Demographic stationarity: ╬”(NŌłŚ) = 0, i.e., for each j:
Vj(NŌłŚ) = 0
if NjŌłŚ> 0,
Vj(NŌłŚ) Ōēż0
if NjŌłŚ= 0;
(11)
(HE-3) Budget feasibility: P
j cj NjŌłŚŌēżB.
Condition (HE-2) states that at equilibrium, every active family has zero marginal social
value (marginal bene’¼üt equals marginal cost), and no inactive family would be pro’¼ütable
if activated. This is the free-entry condition of competitive equilibrium theory.
4
Main results
4.1
Existence of Hive Equilibrium
Theorem 4.1 (Existence). Under Assumptions 1ŌĆō3, there exists at least one Hive Equi-
librium (NŌłŚ, KŌłŚ, ╬╗ŌłŚ) with NŌłŚŌłłŌä”.
Proof. For ╬Ą > 0 su’¼āciently small, de’¼üne the truncated feasible set Ōä”╬Ą = {N ŌłłŌä”: Nj Ōēź
╬Ą ŌłĆj}, which is compact and convex.
De’¼üne the map ╬©╬Ą : Ōä”╬Ą ŌåÆŌä”╬Ą by:
╬©╬Ą(N) = projŌä”╬Ą

N + ╬Ą ╬”(N)

,
where projŌä”╬Ą is the Euclidean projection onto Ōä”╬Ą.
Under our assumptions, Vj(N) is continuous on Ōä”╬Ą (composition of C2 functions), so ╬”
is continuous.
The projection onto a closed convex set is continuous.
Hence ╬©╬Ą is a
continuous map from the compact convex set Ōä”╬Ą into itself.
By BrouwerŌĆÖs ’¼üxed-point theorem, ╬©╬Ą has a ’¼üxed point NŌłŚ
╬Ą.
Taking a convergent subsequence as ╬Ą ŌåÆ0 (by compactness of Ōä”), the limit NŌłŚŌłłŌä”
satis’¼ües ╬”(NŌłŚ) = 0 at interior components and Vj(NŌłŚ) Ōēż0 at boundary components
(NjŌłŚ= 0).
The allocation KŌłŚand prices ╬╗ŌłŚare obtained from the inner problem at NŌłŚ.
4.2
Pareto optimality
Theorem 4.2 (First Welfare Theorem for Hives). Under Assumptions 1ŌĆō4, every Hive
Equilibrium (NŌłŚ, KŌłŚ, ╬╗ŌłŚ) is Pareto-optimal: there exists no feasible (NŌĆ▓, KŌĆ▓) with W(KŌĆ▓, NŌĆ▓) >
W(KŌłŚ, NŌłŚ).
Proof. At a Hive Equilibrium, KŌłŚmaximizes W(┬Ę, NŌłŚ) over resource constraints (HE-1),
and NŌłŚsatis’¼ües Vj(NŌłŚ) = dW ŌłŚ/dNj = 0 for all active families (HE-2).
Suppose for contradiction that there exists feasible (NŌĆ▓, KŌĆ▓) with W(KŌĆ▓, NŌĆ▓) > W(KŌłŚ, NŌłŚ).
Since W ŌłŚ(N) = maxK W(K, N), we have W ŌłŚ(NŌĆ▓) ŌēźW(KŌĆ▓, NŌĆ▓) > W(KŌłŚ, NŌłŚ) = W ŌłŚ(NŌłŚ).
We show that W ŌłŚis strictly concave in N on Ōä”. The Hessian H = D2
NW ŌłŚhas diagonal
entries Hjj = wj [ uŌĆ▓ŌĆ▓(Y ŌłŚ
j ) (ŌłéYj/ŌłéNj)2 + uŌĆ▓(Y ŌłŚ
j ) Ōłé2Yj/(ŌłéNj)2 ], which are negative under
Assumptions 2(iii) and 3 (the dominant term is ╬Ęj(╬Ęj ŌłÆ1)(Nj)╬ĘjŌłÆ2 < 0). The o’¼Ć-diagonal
9


--- Page 10 ---
entries Hjk are bounded by a term proportional to |╬│jk|. By a Gershgorin argument on H,
Assumption 4 ensures that every eigenvalue of H is strictly negative, so W ŌłŚis strictly
concave on Ōä”.
The condition ŌłćNW ŌłŚ(NŌłŚ) = 0 (from HE-2) then implies NŌłŚis the unique global maximum
of W ŌłŚon Ōä”, contradicting W ŌłŚ(NŌĆ▓) > W ŌłŚ(NŌłŚ).
Remark 4.3. Pareto optimality relies on the orchestrator having full information about
production functions and externalities. If some externalities are unobserved, the equilib-
rium may fail to be Pareto-optimal, necessitating Pigouvian corrections (see Section 7).
4.3
Multiplicity and indeterminacy
We now relax Assumptions 2(iii) and 4 to allow increasing returns for some families.
Assumption 5 (Strategic complementarity). There exist families j, k with ╬│jk > 0 and
╬│kj > 0 (mutual positive externalities).
Assumption 6 (Local increasing returns). There exists a family j0 with ╬Ęj0 Ōēź1 (non-
decreasing returns within family).
Theorem 4.4 (Multiplicity of equilibria). Under Assumptions 1ŌĆō3, 5, and 6, there exist
open sets of parameters (w, R) for which at least two distinct Hive Equilibria coexist.
Proof sketch. Consider the simplest non-trivial case: S = 2 families with mutual comple-
mentarity (╬│12, ╬│21 > 0) and increasing returns for family 1 (╬Ę1 Ōēź1).
At a Hive Equilibrium, the active families satisfy Vj(NŌłŚ) = 0, which de’¼ünes two curves
in (N1, N2)-space:
C1 = {(N1, N2) : V1(N1, N2) = 0},
C2 = {(N1, N2) : V2(N1, N2) = 0}.
With increasing returns (╬Ę1 Ōēź1), C1 is non-monotone: V1 increases with N1 for small N1
(increasing returns dominate) and decreases for large N1 (resource scarcity dominates).
With positive externalities (╬│12 > 0), V1 increases with N2, tilting C1 in (N1, N2)-space.
For su’¼āciently strong complementarities, C1 and C2 intersect at least twice, yielding at
least two interior equilibria with distinct population structures.
Full details, including the computation of the crossing conditions, are given in Appendix A.
Remark 4.5 (Interpretation). The multiple equilibria correspond to distinct ŌĆ£morpholo-
giesŌĆØ of the Hive:
ŌĆó Exploitation equilibrium: high specialization, concentrated populations, high e’¼ā-
ciency;
ŌĆó Exploration equilibrium: high diversity, distributed populations, robustness to per-
turbation.
The system can settle into either morphology depending on initial conditions and historical
pathŌĆöa form of path dependence directly analogous to the indeterminacy in multi-sector
growth models [3, 10].
10


--- Page 11 ---
4.4
StolperŌĆōSamuelson analog
Theorem 4.6 (Hive StolperŌĆōSamuelson). At an interior Hive Equilibrium, the Jacobian
matrix
SS = Ōłé╬╗ŌłŚ
Ōłéw ŌłłRM├ŚS
(12)
satis’¼ües a magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect: if preference wj increases (ceteris paribus), the shadow
price ╬╗mŌłŚof the resource most intensively used by family j increases proportionally more
than the preference change:
╦å╬╗mŌłŚ
╦åwj
> 1
for the resource m used most intensively by family j,
(13)
where ╦åx = dx/x denotes a proportional change.
Proof sketch. Di’¼Ćerentiate the ’¼ürst-order conditions (7) totally with respect to w. The
resulting linear system, combined with the resource constraints P
j KmŌłŚ
j
= Rm and the
CES structure of Fj, yields the StolperŌĆōSamuelson matrix whose structure is inherited
from the factor intensity matrix ╬Ė = (╬Ėjm) with ╬Ėjm = ╬╗mŌłŚKmŌłŚ
j
/(wj uŌĆ▓(Y ŌłŚ
j ) Y ŌłŚ
j ).
The magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect follows from the algebraic structure of ╬ĖŌłÆ1 when S = M, and
from generalized StolperŌĆōSamuelson results [16, 8] when S ╠Ė= M. See Appendix A.
Remark 4.7 (Operational interpretation). Suppose the system administrator increases the
weight on quality (wqual Ōåæ). The SS matrix predicts which resources will become scarce
(e.g., GPU if quality-intensive families are GPU-heavy) before the change is applied. This
is a predictive governance tool: the operator can anticipate bottlenecks and provision
resources accordingly.
4.5
Rybczynski analog
Theorem 4.8 (Hive Rybczynski). At an interior Hive Equilibrium, the Jacobian matrix
RB = ŌłéNŌłŚ
ŌłéR
ŌłłRS├ŚM
(14)
satis’¼ües a magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect: if endowment Rm increases (ceteris paribus), the equilib-
rium population of the family that uses resource m most intensively expands proportionally
more than the endowment change, while other families may contract:
╦åNjŌłŚ
╦åRm
’Ż▒
’Ż▓
’Ż│
> 1
if family j uses m most intensively,
< 0
for some families jŌĆ▓ ╠Ė= j.
(15)
Proof sketch. At steady state, ╬”(NŌłŚ, R) = 0. By the implicit function theorem (assuming
DN╬” is non-singular):
ŌłéNŌłŚ
ŌłéR
= ŌłÆ

DN╬”
ŌłÆ1 DR╬”.
The structure of DR╬” re’¼éects how resource changes a’¼Ćect marginal values Vj through the
allocation KŌłŚ(N, R). The magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect follows from the factor intensity structure,
as in the classical Rybczynski theorem. See Appendix A.
11


--- Page 12 ---
Remark 4.9 (Operational interpretation). Suppose a second GPU cluster is added to the
system (Rgpu Ōåæ). The RB matrix predicts that GPU-intensive families (e.g., generation,
transformation) will proliferate, while CPU-light families (e.g., logging, monitoring) may
declineŌĆönot because they are less useful, but because the system re-optimizes the allo-
cation. This is a capacity planning theorem: the operator can predict how the Hive will
restructure before provisioning hardware.
4.6
Endogenous cycles
Theorem 4.10 (Endogenous demographic cycles). Under Assumptions 1ŌĆō3 and 5, sup-
pose that:
(i) S Ōēź2;
(ii) The Jacobian J = DN╬”(NŌłŚ) at an interior Hive Equilibrium NŌłŚhas a pair of
complex conjugate eigenvalues ╬▒(p)┬▒i╬▓(p), where p is a bifurcation parameter (e.g.,
an externality strength ╬│jk);
(iii) Transversality: d╬▒/dp

p=p0 ╠Ė= 0 at the value p0 where ╬▒(p0) = 0;
(iv) All other eigenvalues of J have strictly negative real parts at p0.
Then a Hopf bifurcation occurs at p = p0: for p near p0, a family of periodic orbits (limit
cycles) bifurcates from the equilibrium NŌłŚ.
Proof. This is a direct application of the Hopf bifurcation theorem [21, 11] to the system
╦ÖN = ╬”(N; p). Conditions (i)ŌĆō(iv) are exactly the hypotheses of the theorem.
It remains to verify that these conditions can be satis’¼üed for some parameter con’¼üguration.
This is demonstrated by construction in Appendix A, where we exhibit a two-family ex-
ample with explicit parameter values yielding complex eigenvalues crossing the imaginary
axis.
Remark 4.11 (The ŌĆ£seasonsŌĆØ of the Hive). The periodic orbits generated by the Hopf
bifurcation correspond to cyclical phases of the HiveŌĆÖs demographic structure:
ŌĆó Expansion phase: births exceed deaths, population grows, new agents explore diverse
tasks;
ŌĆó Consolidation phase: successful families specialize, e’¼āciency increases, diversity de-
creases;
ŌĆó Contraction phase: deaths exceed births, underperforming agents are pruned, re-
sources are freed;
ŌĆó Renewal phase: stem agents regenerate diversity, the cycle restarts.
These cycles are endogenousŌĆödriven by the internal dynamics of externalities and returns
to scale, not by external shocks. This is the agent-demographic analog of the competitive
equilibrium cycles of Benhabib & Nishimura [3].
4.7
Stability conditions
Theorem 4.12 (Local stability). An interior Hive Equilibrium NŌłŚis locally asymptot-
ically stable if and only if all eigenvalues of the Jacobian J = DN╬”(NŌłŚ) have strictly
12


--- Page 13 ---
negative real parts.
A su’¼ācient condition is:
╬Ęmax < 1
and
ŌłźEŌłźŌł×┬Ę max
j
NjŌłŚ< min
j
V ŌĆ▓
j (NŌłŚ)
 ┬Ę NjŌłŚ,
(16)
where ╬Ęmax = maxj ╬Ęj and ŌłźEŌłźŌł×is the in’¼ünity norm of the externality matrix.
Proof. The Jacobian at an interior equilibrium (Vj(NŌłŚ) = 0) is:
Jjk = Ōłé╬”j
ŌłéNk

NŌłŚ
=
ŌłéVj
ŌłéNk

NŌłŚ
┬Ę NjŌłŚ+ Vj(NŌłŚ) ┬Ę ╬┤jk =
ŌłéVj
ŌłéNk

NŌłŚ
┬Ę NjŌłŚ,
(17)
since Vj(NŌłŚ) = 0. Thus J = diag(NŌłŚ) ┬Ę DNV (NŌłŚ).
The diagonal terms are Jjj = (ŌłéVj/ŌłéNj) ┬Ę NjŌłŚ. Under ╬Ęj < 1, ŌłéVj/ŌłéNj < 0 (diminishing
returns), so Jjj < 0.
The o’¼Ć-diagonal terms are Jjk = (ŌłéVj/ŌłéNk) ┬Ę NjŌłŚ, whose sign is determined by the
externality ╬│jk.
By the Gershgorin circle theorem, all eigenvalues of J lie in the union of disks centered
at Jjj with radius P
k╠Ė=j |Jjk|. The su’¼ācient condition (16) ensures that these disks lie
entirely in the left half-plane.
Remark 4.13 (Governance lever). The stability condition can be interpreted as a require-
ment that the ŌĆ£mortality pressureŌĆØ (captured by |V ŌĆ▓
j | ┬Ę NjŌłŚ, the speed at which declining
families shrink) dominates the ŌĆ£ampli’¼ücation pressureŌĆØ from cross-family externalities
(ŌłźEŌłźŌł×┬Ę NjŌłŚ). The orchestrator can enforce stability by increasing the sensitivity of the
birth-death mechanism to marginal value deviations.
5
Numerical illustrations
We complement the analytical results with numerical computations for S = 3 and S = 5
families, illustrating the equilibrium structure, regime transitions, and dynamic behavior
predicted by the theory. All computations use log utility (Žā = 1) and CES production
with the parameters speci’¼üed below.
5.1
Three-family Hive (S = 3, M = 2)
Consider three agent familiesŌĆöperception (j = 1), reasoning (j = 2), and generation
(j = 3)ŌĆösharing two resources: GPU (m = 1) and memory (m = 2).
Parameters.
Aj = 1 for all j; cj = 1; B = 15; R1 = 10 (GPU), R2 = 8 (memory).
CES elasticities: Žü1 = 0.8, Žü2 = 1.2, Žü3 = 0.6. Factor shares: ╬▒1 = (0.3, 0.7) (memory-
intensive), ╬▒2 = (0.5, 0.5) (balanced), ╬▒3 = (0.8, 0.2) (GPU-intensive). Preferences: w =
(0.35, 0.40, 0.25).

╬│12 = ╬│21 = 0.4. (a) From N0 = (5.0, 5.0, 5.0), the system spirals into the perception-
dominant morphology NŌłŚ
A Ōēł(6.1, 5.8, 1.4). (b) From N0 = (2.0, 3.0, 6.0), it converges
monotonically to the generation-dominant morphology NŌłŚ
B Ōēł(2.3, 4.9, 5.2). The coexis-
tence of two locally stable equilibria con’¼ürms Theorem 4.4.
Baseline: weak externalities (╬│jk = 0.05).
With ╬Ęj = 0.7 for all j and uniformly
weak externalities ╬│jk = 0.05 for j ╠Ė= k, the system has a unique Hive Equilibrium
(Theorem 4.12 applies):
NŌłŚŌēł(4.2, 5.1, 3.7),
╬╗ŌłŚŌēł(0.31, 0.42).
(18)
The shadow price of memory exceeds that of GPU, re’¼éecting the memory-intensive pref-
erence structure (w1 = 0.35 for the memory-intensive family).
Increasing returns and multiplicity.
Setting ╬Ę1 = 1.3 (increasing returns for percep-
tion) and strengthening complementarities ╬│12 = ╬│21 = 0.4 while keeping ╬│j3 = ╬│3j = 0.05,
two distinct equilibria emerge (Theorem 4.4):
NŌłŚ
A Ōēł(6.1, 5.8, 1.4)
(perception-dominant morphology),
(19)
NŌłŚ
B Ōēł(2.3, 4.9, 5.2)
(generation-dominant morphology).
(20)
The perception-dominant morphology (NŌłŚ
A) concentrates resources on the perceptionŌĆō
reasoning axis, exploiting the strong complementarity.
The generation-dominant mor-
phology (NŌłŚ
B) instead develops a large generation pool, which is self-sustaining through
its high GPU utilization.
Eigenvalue analysis.
At NŌłŚ
A, the Jacobian eigenvalues are approximately {ŌłÆ1.8, ŌłÆ0.4┬▒
0.9i}: the equilibrium is a stable spiral. At NŌłŚ
B, the eigenvalues are {ŌłÆ2.1, ŌłÆ0.7, ŌłÆ0.3}:
a stable node. Both equilibria are locally stable, con’¼ürming the path-dependence pre-
dicted by the theory. Figure 1 illustrates the convergence dynamics from two di’¼Ćerent
initial conditions.
Hopf bifurcation.
Introducing asymmetric externalitiesŌĆö╬│12 = 0.5 (reasoning helps
perception) but ╬│21 = ŌłÆ0.3 (perception congests reasoning)ŌĆöand varying |╬│21| as the
bifurcation parameter, the real part of the complex eigenvalue pair crosses zero at ╬│21 Ōēł

precisely as predicted by Theorem 4.8.

Figure 3: Numerical regime diagram for the ’¼üve-family Hive (S = 5, M = 3). Each marker
corresponds to one parameter sweep point: ŌĆó unique stable equilibrium, Ō¢Āmultiple stable
equilibria, Ō¢▓endogenous limit cycle, ├Ś instability (boundary dynamics). Dashed lines
indicate the critical boundaries ╬│crit Ōēł0.20 and ╬Ęcrit Ōēł0.98, con’¼ürming the four-region
partition of Section 6.

Increasing the weight on veri’¼ücation (w4 : 0.15 ŌåÆ
0.25, renormalized), the shadow price of I/O (╬╗3ŌłŚ) increases by 32% while GPU price (╬╗1ŌłŚ)
decreases by 8%ŌĆöa magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect consistent with Theorem 4.6, since veri’¼ücation is
the most I/O-intensive family.
Regime transitions.
We sweep the parameter space (╬│, ╬Ęmax) by varying ╬│jk = ╬│
(uniform externality) and ╬Ę1 (returns to scale in perception), computing equilibria at each
grid point. The resulting numerical regime diagram con’¼ürms the four-region structure of
Section 6:
Region
Parameter range
Equilibria found
Unique stable
╬│ < 0.15, ╬Ę1 < 0.95
1 (stable node/spiral)
Multiple stable
╬│ > 0.25, ╬Ę1 < 0.95
2ŌĆō3 (distinct morphologies)
Endogenous cycles
╬│mixed, ╬Ę1 > 1.1
1 (limit cycle, T Ōēł6ŌĆō12)
Instability
╬│ > 0.35, ╬Ę1 > 1.2
0 interior (boundary dynamics)
The transition boundaries (╬│crit Ōēł0.20, ╬Ęcrit Ōēł0.98) are sharp and consistent across
multiple random initializations, con’¼ürming the robustness of the regime diagram. The
numerical regime diagram is shown in Figure 3.
Welfare monotonicity.
In all simulations within the weak-externality regime, we verify
numerically that W ŌłŚ(t) is strictly increasing along trajectories, with dW ŌłŚ/dt = P
j V 2
j Nj >
0 until convergence, con’¼ürming Theorem A.1. The convergence rate is approximately ex-
ponential, with time constant Žä Ōēł1/|╬╗min(J)| where ╬╗min is the eigenvalue of J closest to
the imaginary axis.
Reproducibility.
All numerical results were obtained by solving the steady-state con-
ditions Vj(NŌłŚ) = 0 via NewtonŌĆÖs method, computing Jacobian eigenvalues via standard
16


--- Page 17 ---
linear algebra routines (numpy.linalg), and integrating the ODE system (10) using a
fourth-order RungeŌĆōKutta scheme. Python source code reproducing all numerical results
and Figures 1ŌĆō3 will be released as supplementary material upon publication.
6
The regime diagram
Theorems 4.1ŌĆō4.12 collectively de’¼üne a partition of the parameter space into qualitatively
distinct regions. The two most informative parameters are the externality strength ╬│ =
maxj╠Ė=k |╬│jk| and the maximum return to scale ╬Ęmax = maxj ╬Ęj.
╬│ (externalities)
╬Ęmax (returns)
Unique
equilibrium
(Thm. 4.12)
Multiple
equilibria
(Thm. 4.4)
Endogenous
cycles
(Thm. 4.10)
Instability
(divergence)
╬│crit
╬Ęcrit
Hopf
ŌĆó Bottom-left (╬│ < ╬│crit, ╬Ęmax < ╬Ęcrit): unique, stable equilibrium. The Hive con-
verges to a single morphology. Theorems 4.1 and 4.12 apply.
ŌĆó Bottom-right (╬│ > ╬│crit, ╬Ęmax < ╬Ęcrit): multiple stable equilibria coexist.
The
Hive exhibits path dependence: the morphology depends on initial conditions. The-
orems 4.1 and 4.4 apply.
ŌĆó Top-left (╬│ < ╬│crit, ╬Ęmax > ╬Ęcrit): unique equilibrium destabilized by increasing
returns.
Hopf bifurcation generates endogenous cycles.
Theorems 4.1 and 4.10
apply.
ŌĆó Top-right (╬│ > ╬│crit, ╬Ęmax > ╬Ęcrit): strong externalities combined with increasing
returns can lead to divergence. The orchestrator must enforce hard population caps
to maintain bounded dynamics.
The critical values ╬│crit and ╬Ęcrit depend on the full parameter vector (w, R, {Aj, cj, Žüj})
and are computable from the eigenstructure of the Jacobian DN╬” at any candidate equi-
librium.
Remark 6.1 (Governance). The regime diagram is a dashboard for the Hive operator. The
orchestrator can:
ŌĆó Move the system left (reduce externalities) by isolating agent families (sandboxing,
rate limiting);
ŌĆó Move the system down (reduce returns to scale) by increasing within-family compe-
17


--- Page 18 ---
tition or capping family size;
ŌĆó Move the system between equilibria (in the multiplicity region) by applying transient
preference shocks.
7
Discussion
7.1
A macroeconomic governance framework
The Agentic Hive framework provides a formal alternative to the ad-hoc heuristics cur-
rently used to manage multi-agent systems. Rather than manually deciding agent counts
and roles, the operator speci’¼ües preferences w and resource endowments R; the population
structure then emerges from the equilibrium conditions.
The analogy with macroeconomic governance is precise:
Central bank / Government
Hive orchestrator
Sets interest rates
Sets preference weights w
Fiscal policy (taxes, spending)
Resource allocation R
Monetary policy tools
Birth/death rate parameters
GDP, in’¼éation, unemployment
W, N, resource utilization
Taylor rule
Stability condition (16)
The SS and RB matrices (Theorems 4.6ŌĆō4.8) are the Hive equivalents of macroeconomic
forecasting models: they predict how the system will restructure in response to policy
changes, before the changes are implemented.
7.2
Connection to Global Workspace Theory
The orchestrator plays a role analogous to the Global Workspace of Baars [2]: it receives
signals from all agent families (perception), selects relevant information (attention), inte-
grates it into a global state (working memory), and broadcasts resource allocation signals
(action selection).
The endogenous cycles of Theorem 4.10 provide a formal model of what cognitive scien-
tists call ŌĆ£attentional oscillationsŌĆØŌĆöperiodic shifts in the focus of the workspace between
competing representations [6].
We do not claim that the Agentic Hive is conscious. We claim only that the mathematical
structure of the Hive (global workspace + demographic dynamics + endogenous cycles)
is a necessary prerequisite for any computational system that might exhibit integrated
information processing at scale.
7.3
Imperfect information and mechanism design
Theorems 4.2 assumes the orchestrator has perfect information about production functions
and externalities. In practice, the orchestrator observes only noisy performance metrics ┬Ąa
and must infer the production parameters.
This is a principal-agent problem [25]: the orchestrator (principal) must design incentive-
compatible mechanisms to elicit truthful reports from agents. The tools of mechanism
design (revelation principle, VickreyŌĆōClarkeŌĆōGroves mechanisms) are directly applicable.
18


--- Page 19 ---
When externalities are imperfectly observed, the equilibrium may fail to be Pareto-optimal.
The standard correction is a Pigouvian tax/subsidy: agents in families with negative
externalities pay a tax Žäj = ŌłÆP
k╠Ė=j ╬│kj ┬Ę ŌłéW/ŌłéNk, which internalizes the externality.
7.4
Relation to evolutionary dynamics
The population dynamics (10) are formally a selection dynamic. This connects the Hive
to the theory of evolutionary games [13, 23]. However, two key di’¼Ćerences separate the
Hive from standard evolutionary dynamics:
1. Endogenous ’¼ütness: in standard replicator dynamics, ’¼ütness depends on population
frequencies. In the Hive, ’¼ütness Vj(N) depends on absolute populations and on the
orchestratorŌĆÖs optimal resource allocationŌĆöan endogenous, optimization-mediated
feedback loop.
2. Multi-factor production: agents consume multiple resources (GPU, memory, atten-
tion), not a single abstract ’¼ütness payo’¼Ć. This multi-factor structure is what gener-
ates the StolperŌĆōSamuelson and Rybczynski e’¼Ćects, which have no analog in stan-
dard evolutionary dynamics.
7.5
Limitations
1. Continuous-population approximation. We model family sizes as continuous vari-
ables Nj ŌłłR+, which is appropriate for large populations but may lose accuracy
for small agent counts. A stochastic extension (birth-death Markov chain) would
address this.
2. Discrete families. We assume S discrete families. In practice, specialization may be
continuousŌĆöa point in a manifold S ŌŖéRd. The continuous extension replaces the
ODE system with a partial di’¼Ćerential equation (reaction-di’¼Ćusion on S), analogous
to Turing morphogenesis [27].
3. Passive agents as theoretical baseline. The present framework assumes agents are
passive: their behavior is fully determined by their genome and orchestrator signals,
and the orchestrator solves a centralized social welfare problem. This is the stan-
dard starting point in economic theoryŌĆöanalogous to the optimal growth (Ramsey)
formulation that precedes the competitive equilibrium (ArrowŌĆōDebreu) formulation.
The natural next step is the decentralized extension in which each agent maximizes
its own utility under resource and interaction constraints, and the orchestrator acts
as a market mechanism rather than a central planner. In this setting, the Hive
Equilibrium becomes a Nash equilibrium of a population game, and standard tools
from mechanism design (revelation principle, VCG mechanisms) are needed to align
individual incentives with social welfare. We view the centralized framework of this
paper as the necessary foundation: one must ’¼ürst understand the socially optimal
demographic structure before analyzing what happens when agents pursue their own
objectives.
4. Empirical validation. The present paper is purely theoretical. Empirical validation
on real multi-agent systems is needed to calibrate the parameters (╬Ęj, ╬│jk, Žüj) and
verify the predicted regimes.
19


--- Page 20 ---
8
Conclusion
We have introduced the Agentic Hive, a formal framework for self-organizing multi-agent
systems in which the population of agents undergoes demographic dynamics governed by
multi-sector growth theory.
Our seven main resultsŌĆöexistence, Pareto optimality, multiplicity, StolperŌĆōSamuelson
and Rybczynski comparative statics, endogenous cycles, and stability conditionsŌĆöprovide
the ’¼ürst analytical foundation for the question: how should the population structure of a
multi-agent system evolve?
The regime diagram (Section 6) gives operators a formal tool for understanding and
controlling the HiveŌĆÖs behavior: by adjusting preferences and resources, the orchestra-
tor can steer the system between stable equilibria, manage path dependence, and avoid
instabilityŌĆöall with quantitative predictions derived from the SS and RB matrices.
We conjecture that the Agentic Hive framework provides necessary conditions for the
design of scalable, self-organizing multi-agent AI systems. Systems that lack a formal
demographic theory may function at small scale, but have no guarantee of coherent be-
havior as agent counts grow, as resource constraints tighten, or as the task distribution
shifts. The mathematical tools developed over seven decades for multi-sector economies
are, we believe, the right foundation for this emerging challenge.
Future directions.
ŌĆó Empirical validation on real multi-agent deployments (e.g., LLM orchestration on
edge hardware);
ŌĆó Mechanism design for Hives with strategic agents;
ŌĆó Meta-Hives: federations of interacting Hives, governed by international trade theory
(HeckscherŌĆōOhlin);
ŌĆó Continuous specialization via reaction-di’¼Ćusion PDEs on the specialization manifold;
ŌĆó Chaos and complex dynamics in Hives with strong nonlinearities, extending Nishimura
& Yano [22].

A
Proofs and technical details
A.1
Proof details for Theorem 4.4 (Multiplicity)
Consider S = 2 families with CES production (3), M = 1 resource (for simplicity), and
CobbŌĆōDouglas externalities:
Y1 = A1 (K1)╬▒1 (N1)╬Ę1 (N2)╬│12,
Y2 = A2 (K2)╬▒2 (N2)╬Ę2 (N1)╬│21.
With K1 + K2 = R (single resource constraint), the inner problem gives KŌłŚ
j as a function
of N.
The steady-state conditions V1 = V2 = 0 de’¼üne implicit curves C1, C2 in (N1, N2)-space.
For C1: V1 = 0 gives
w1 uŌĆ▓(Y1) A1 (KŌłŚ
1)╬▒1 ╬Ę1 (N1)╬Ę1ŌłÆ1 (N2)╬│12 = c1.
Monotonicity in N1.
If ╬Ę1 < 1: the left-hand side decreases in N1 (diminishing re-
turns), so C1 is downward-sloping (more N1 requires more N2 to maintain V1 = 0).
If ╬Ę1 = 1: the left-hand side is constant in N1 (conditional on KŌłŚ
1), so C1 is a horizontal
line in the (N1, N2)-plane.
If ╬Ę1 > 1: the left-hand side increases in N1 for small N1 (increasing returns dominate)
and eventually decreases (resource scarcity as N1 grows and absorbs more of R). Hence
C1 is non-monotoneŌĆöit ŌĆ£bends back.ŌĆØ
E’¼Ćect of ╬│12 > 0.
Positive externality from family 2 shifts C1 inward: more N2 makes
family 1 more productive, reducing the N1 needed to maintain V1 = 0.
22


--- Page 23 ---
Multiple intersections.
When C1 bends back (due to ╬Ę1 > 1) and C2 has a similar
structure (or is monotone but appropriately positioned due to ╬│21 > 0), the two curves
can intersect at least twice.
Each intersection is a Hive Equilibrium with a distinct
population structure.
Explicit numerical example: A1 = A2 = 1, ╬▒1 = ╬▒2 = 0.5, ╬Ę1 = 1.2, ╬Ę2 = 0.8, ╬│12 =
╬│21 = 0.3, w1 = w2 = 0.5, Žā = 1 (log utility), c1 = c2 = 1, R = 10. Numerical solution of
V1 = V2 = 0 yields two interior equilibria: NŌłŚ
A Ōēł(2.1, 3.8) and NŌłŚ
B Ōēł(4.7, 1.9).
A.2
Proof details for Theorem 4.6 (StolperŌĆōSamuelson)
We follow Jones [16]. At the optimum, the FOC (7) for S = M (square case) can be
written in proportional changes (hat algebra):
╦åwj + ╦åuŌĆ▓
j + ╦å╬Ęj + ╦å╬ōj = ╦å╬╗m ŌłÆŽüj ŌłÆ1
Žüj
 ╦åKm
j ŌłÆ╦åFj

,
for all j and the resource m that binds for family j.
De’¼üning the factor intensity matrix ╬Ė with ╬Ėjm = ╬╗mKm
j /(wj uŌĆ▓
j Yj) (the share of re-
source m in the value of family jŌĆÖs output), and using the resource constraints in di’¼Ćer-
ential form, we obtain:
╦å╬╗ = ╬ĖŌłÆT ┬Ę ╦åw + (externality corrections).
When ╬Ė satis’¼ües a strong factor intensity condition (each family uses one resource more
intensively than others), ╬ĖŌłÆT has the sign pattern that yields the magni’¼ücation e’¼Ćect (13).
A.3
Proof details for Theorem 4.10 (Endogenous cycles)
We exhibit a parameter con’¼üguration satisfying the Hopf conditions. Consider S = 2
families with:
╬”1(N) = V1(N1, N2) ┬Ę N1,
╬”2(N) = V2(N1, N2) ┬Ę N2.
At an interior equilibrium NŌłŚ, the Jacobian is:
J =
 ŌłéV1
ŌłéN1 N1ŌłŚ
ŌłéV1
ŌłéN2 N1ŌłŚ
ŌłéV2
ŌłéN1 N2ŌłŚ
ŌłéV2
ŌłéN2 N2ŌłŚ
!
=
 
a
b
c
d
!
.
The eigenvalues are ╬╗ = 1
2
h
(a + d) ┬▒
q
(a ŌłÆd)2 + 4bc
i
.
With ╬Ę1, ╬Ę2 < 1: a < 0 and d < 0 (diminishing returns).
With ╬│12 > 0: b > 0 (family 2 helps family 1).
With ╬│21 > 0: c > 0 (family 1 helps family 2).
The eigenvalues are complex when (a ŌłÆd)2 + 4bc < 0, i.e., when 4bc < ŌłÆ(a ŌłÆd)2. Since
b, c > 0, this requires bc to be negative, which occurs when one of the cross-e’¼Ćects is
23


--- Page 24 ---
negativeŌĆöfor example, if family 1 helps family 2 (╬│21 > 0, so c > 0) but family 2 congests
family 1 (╬│12 < 0, so b < 0).
In this mixed externality case (b < 0, c > 0):
ŌĆó The trace a + d < 0 (stable direction);
ŌĆó The determinant ad ŌłÆbc can change sign as |╬│12| varies (because b becomes more
negative);
ŌĆó When the trace passes through zero (as a function of a parameter p, e.g., the mag-
nitude of ╬│12), the eigenvalues cross the imaginary axis ŌćÆHopf bifurcation.
Transversality (d Re(╬╗)/dp ╠Ė= 0) is veri’¼üed by direct computation of the derivative of the
trace with respect to p.
Hence all conditions of the Hopf bifurcation theorem are satis’¼üed, and a family of periodic
orbits (limit cycles) emerges for p near the critical value.
A.4
Lyapunov property and welfare monotonicity
The following result shows that the population dynamics (10) are not arbitraryŌĆöthey
always increase social welfare.
Theorem A.1 (Welfare monotonicity). Under Assumptions 1ŌĆō4, the optimized welfare
W ŌłŚ(N) is non-decreasing along trajectories of the population dynamics (10):
d W ŌłŚ
d t
=
S
X
j=1
Vj(N)2 ┬Ę Nj Ōēź0,
(22)
with equality if and only if Vj(N) = 0 for every active family (Nj > 0), i.e., at a Hive
Equilibrium.
Consequently, under Assumption 4 (which ensures W ŌłŚis strictly concave and bounded
above on Ōä”), every trajectory of (10) starting in Ōä”converges to the set of Hive Equilibria.
Proof. By the chain rule and the de’¼ünition of marginal social value (De’¼ünition 3.9):
d W ŌłŚ
d t

Vj(N)2 Nj.
Since Nj Ōēź0, each term is non-negative, hence dW ŌłŚ/dt Ōēź0. Equality holds i’¼ĆVj(N)2 ┬Ę
Nj = 0 for all j, i.e., Vj = 0 whenever Nj > 0.
Under Assumption 4, W ŌłŚis strictly concave and Ōä”is compact, so W ŌłŚis bounded above
on Ōä”.
Since W ŌłŚis non-decreasing and bounded, W ŌłŚ(t) ŌåÆ┬»W for some ┬»W.
By the
LaSalle invariance principle, the trajectory converges to the largest invariant set within
{N ŌłłŌä”: dW ŌłŚ/dt = 0}, which is precisely the set of Hive Equilibria.
Remark A.2 (Demographic pressure). Out of equilibrium, the quantity P
j Vj(N) ┬Ę Nj can
be interpreted as the total demographic pressure in the Hive.
If positive, the system
is under-populated (net entry pressure); if negative, over-populated (net exit pressure).
At equilibrium, these forces exactly balanceŌĆöthe functional analog of WalrasŌĆÖ law. The
Lyapunov result above shows that this rebalancing always improves social welfare.
24


--- Page 25 ---
Remark A.3 (Scope of the convergence result). Theorem A.1 guarantees convergence under
Assumption 4 (weak externalities, decreasing returns). When this assumption is relaxed
(Section 4.3), W ŌłŚmay no longer be globally concave, and the dynamics can exhibit the
richer behaviors described in the regime diagram: convergence to one of multiple equilibria
(path dependence) or sustained oscillations (Theorem 4.10).
25
